a summary of my views on mental health: if you're halfway functional, problems like depression, anxiety, ADHD etc are healable. even curable
ofc, it's hard, takes a long time & a lot of resources. if you can, find a therapist who thinks issues can be overcome & will help you do it
when i was a kid, various doctors diagnosed me with depression, ADHD, bipolar disorder, anxiety disorder and asperger's syndrome
but those aren't concrete illnesses like having a flu virus in your bloodstream. they're labels for symptoms and behaviors which can change
if you learn to socialize, make eye contact and recognize people's emotional states, no doctor will say you "have" asperger's anymore

The two biggest stars to come from Canadian stand-up were Jim Carrey and Howie Mandel. Before my time, but both hysterical and fine men.
There were Simon Rackoff and Howard Nemetz, who were writing partners but also comics in the great old-timey style I love so much.
There was Lou Eisen, who looked and talked like Groucho. He lived for comedy.
There was Brent Piaskowski who decided I would be a good writing partner, but I was awful. He went on to be a great sitcom writer.
There were the team of Al and George, a punk Smothers Brothers. They'd take there pay in singles, throw it in the air and fight for payment
There was Derek Edwards, the most Canadian of any comic ever, comedy's equivalent of Stomping Tom Connors. A living folk legend.
There was Howie Wagman who loved comedians and listened to them and helped them and was wonderful.
There was Donnie Coy, who owned Hamilton, and could wear the hell out of a hat.

There were great French-Canadian comedians, and I will speak of them later in their native tongue.
There was Ron Vaudry, a great and generous guy, funny as hell.
There was Jim McAleese, who made me laugh as much as anybody ever.
There was Larry Horowitz, by far the most professional of all the comedians. Always killed with an airtight set.
But being a Canadian comedian in that time was like nothing else. Those who were there then know that. I will explain the feeling later.
It was a feeling that only about 50 people in the world have shared.
There was John Wing, a very deliberate, very precise wordsmith, whose jokes were as meticulous as his wardrobe.
There were many more and I am growing weary but I will tell many stories over the next few weeks on these great men.
There was Ronnie Edwards. Good night for now.
